summary: Uncle Bob explores whether Test Driven Development remains valid when programming in a functional style, using a word wrap algorithm in Clojure as his demonstration. He builds the solution incrementally through six carefully crafted test cases, showing that TDD works naturally with functional programming. He then contrasts his TDD approach with a more 'transformational' functional style, finding the latter more complex and less elegant for this problem. His attempt to build the transformational version without TDD took three times longer and involved painful debugging, reinforcing his conviction that discipline transcends paradigm shifts.
key_points: TDD applies to functional programming just as well as to object-oriented or procedural programming
Not all functional programs need to be sequences of transformations - simpler is still better
Well-ordered, carefully considered, extremely minimal test cases are valuable but take practice to write well
Abandoning TDD for manual REPL-based development led to longer development time and painful debugging
Changing paradigms doesn't mean throwing out fundamental disciplines that work
money_quotes: Most people wouldn't write them that way. It took me a lot of time with TDD before I realized the value of well-ordered, carefully considered, extremely minimal test cases.
So, as you start to explore functional programming, don't throw out the baby with the bathwater. Keep your TDD discipline!
The old rule of 'simpler is better' still applies.

The entire process took at least three times longer than the TDD approach, involved a lot of nasty debugging and print statements, and caused many infinite recursive loops that blew the stack and crashed my console.
Functional programming may be a change - an important change; but it doesn't change everything.
Programming is still programming. Discipline is still discipline.
There may be some rules that need to be discarded when you adopt functional programming; but so far I haven't found any.
key_insight: When adopting new programming paradigms, fundamental disciplines like TDD remain valuable—don't abandon proven practices just because the syntax changes.
